sessions.lever: val|cmd OR operand source order (pure-C, no pins)
sessions.notes: Removing register-asm pins (cmd asm v1, val asm v0) and writing `*(u32*)(a0+4) = val | cmd` (val first in OR source) yields val→$v0, cmd→$v1 — target's register allocation. The remaining 1-byte (4-byte instruction) diff is the OR operand order: build emits `or v0,v0,v1` (00431025), target has `or v0,v1,v0` (00621025). Both encode the same commutative OR but to different bytes.

remaining_gap: OR operand-order commutativity diff. `cmd | val` source order would emit `or v0,v1,v0` (target's bytes) IF val landed in $v0. But in `cmd | val` source, GCC's combine pass folds val's andi-destination into $a3 (a3 dies after val's andi, so combine reuses it), giving `or v0,v0,a3` — wrong register AND wrong operand order.
next_hypotheses: Find a pure-C lever that keeps a3 alive past val's andi without emitting extra instructions and without falling into the forbidden 'cheats by any spelling' family (no dead self-assign, no `(void)a3`, no `a3 & 0` style coercions, no volatile cast).
Look for an unrelated downstream use of a3 in the original source that's been lost in decomp (e.g., a debug log call, a sibling store of the raw mode value).
Read GCC 2.7.2's combine.c try_combine path to see whether ANY pure-C-derivable RTL shape prevents the dest-substitution of val→a3.

rejected_forms.reason: val folds into a3 in cmd|val source order
reordering val computation before cmd doesn't prevent val→a3 fold
val=a3; val&=X combines back to val=a3&X
using a3 &= X; a3 |= ... directly puts result in a3
u32 val=a3&X at decl with later ifs adds prologue insns
